title: Physical scientists
description: Conduct research in the genetics, nutrition, reproduction, growth, and development of domestic farm animals. Use chemistry, microbiology, engineering, and other sciences to study the principles underlying the processing and deterioration of foods; analyze food content to determine levels of vitamins, fat, sugar, and protein; discover new food sources; research ways to make processed foods safe, palatable, and healthful; and apply food science knowledge to determine best ways to process, package, preserve, store, and distribute food. Conduct research in breeding, physiology, production, yield, and management of crops and agricultural plants or trees, shrubs, and nursery stock, their growth in soils, and control of pests; or study the chemical, physical, biological, and mineralogical composition of soils as they relate to plant or crop growth. May classify and map soils and investigate effects of alternative practices on soil and crop productivity. (Based on 23 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Apply research findings to help minimize the environmental impacts of pollution, waterborne diseases, erosion, and sedimentation.
Collaborate with medical or health researchers to address health problems related to geological materials or processes.
Coordinate and supervise the work of professional and technical staff, including research assistants, technologists, and technicians.

Analyze and interpret geological data, using computer software; prepare geological maps, cross-sectional diagrams, charts, or reports concerning mineral extraction, land use, or resource management, using results of fieldwork or laboratory research.

Develop computer models for hydrologic predictions; formulate predictions by interpreting environmental data, such as meteorological, atmospheric, oceanic, paleoclimate, climate, or related information.
Prepare written and oral reports describing research results, using illustrations, maps, appendices, and other information.

Analyze climate data sets using techniques such as geophysical fluid dynamics, data assimilation, or numerical modeling; conduct geological or geophysical studies to provide information for use in regional development, site selection, or development of public works projects; conduct short- and long-term climate assessments and study storm occurrences.
Communicate geological findings by writing research papers, participating in conferences, or teaching geological science at universities.
Advise construction firms or government agencies on dam or road construction, foundation design, land use, or resource management.

Study and document quantities, distribution, disposition, and development of underground and surface waters; investigate complaints or conflicts related to the alteration of public waters, gathering information, recommending alternatives, informing participants of progress, and preparing draft orders; measure characteristics of the Earth, such as gravity or magnetic fields, using equipment such as seismographs, gravimeters, torsion balances, or magnetometers; analyze research data to determine its significance, using computers.

Gather data from sources such as surface or upper air stations, satellites, weather bureaus, or radar for use in meteorological reports or forecasts; prepare forecasts or briefings to meet the needs of industry, business, government, or other groups.

Evaluate research data in terms of its impact on issues such as soil and water conservation, flood control planning, and water supply forecasting; prepare hydrogeologic evaluations of known or suspected hazardous waste sites and land treatment and feedlot facilities; design and conduct scientific hydrogeological investigations to ensure that accurate and appropriate information is available for use in water resource management decisions; study public water supply issues, including flood and drought risks, water quality, wastewater, and impacts on wetland habitats.

Plan or conduct geological, geochemical, or geophysical field studies or surveys, sample collection, or drilling and testing programs used to collect data for research or application; collect and analyze water samples as part of field investigations or to validate data from automatic monitors; document activities and record information, such as the numbers of applications completed, presentations conducted, and persons assisted.

Investigate the composition, structure, or history of the Earth's crust through the collection, examination, measurement, or classification of soils, minerals, rocks, or fossil remains; develop theories based on personal observations or on observations and theories of other astronomers; analyze data from research conducted to detect and measure physical phenomena; collaborate with other scientists in the design, development, and testing of experimental, industrial, or medical equipment, instrumentation, and procedures.

Collaborate with other astronomers to carry out research projects; teach astronomy or astrophysics; perform managerial duties, such as creating work schedules, creating or implementing staff training, matching staff expertise to situations, or analyzing performance of offices; and serve on professional panels and committees.

Review environmental, historical, or technical reports and publications for accuracy; and inspect specified areas to ensure the presence of fire prevention equipment, safety equipment, or first-aid supplies.

Develop, improve, or customize products, equipment, formulas, processes, or analytical methods; conduct quality control tests; analyze incident data to identify trends in injuries, illnesses, accidents, or other hazards.

Develop operational plans and policies necessary to achieve health education objectives and services, including preparing and distributing health education materials such as reports, bulletins, and visual aids to address smoking, vaccines, and other public health concerns.

Develop strategies for more environmentally friendly resource extraction and reclamation; research the impact of industrial projects or pollution on climate, air quality, or weather phenomena; and identify risks for natural disasters, such as mudslides, earthquakes, or volcanic eruptions.

Conduct wind assessment, integration, or validation studies; create visualizations to illustrate historical or future changes in the Earth's climate; and conduct numerical simulations of climate conditions to understand and predict global or regional weather patterns.

Perform laboratory analyses or physical inspections of samples to detect disease or to assess purity or cleanliness; maintain or update emergency response plans or procedures; investigate health-related complaints and inspect facilities to ensure that they comply with public health legislation and regulations.

Prepare and distribute health education materials, such as reports, bulletins, and visual aids, to address smoking, vaccines, and other public health concerns; develop educational materials and programs for community agencies, local government, and state government; maintain inventories of hazardous materials or hazardous wastes.
Research and conduct studies on the genetics, nutrition, and management of domesticated farm animals to improve efficiency and quality, including animal breeding and feeding practices.
Conduct research in food science, including the development of new food items, safety assessments, and nutritional analyses, while staying up to date on regulations and scientific literature.
Design and monitor laboratory experiments aimed at understanding biological processes, including genetic research methods and cellular or molecular biology studies.

Compile and analyze biological data to investigate the relationship between organisms and their environment, including studies on pest management and the impact of agriculture practices on ecosystems.
Write research papers, grant proposals, and technical reports to communicate findings and seek funding for ongoing scientific research.
Supervise laboratory staff and technicians while ensuring adherence to safety protocols and efficient lab operations.
Collaborate with professionals to perform experiments and analyze data regarding animal health, disease prevention, and quality control in agricultural products.
Evaluate and interpret experimental findings related to genetics and biological processes, contributing to advancements in agricultural productivity and food safety.
Instruct and supervise students in biological sciences at the university level, including guiding their research projects and enhancing their practical skills.
Study the physical principles of living cells or organisms and their electrical or mechanical energy, applying methods and knowledge of mathematics, physics, chemistry, or biology.
Isolate, analyze, or synthesize vitamins, hormones, allergens, minerals, or enzymes and determine their effects on body functions.

Test new products for flavor, texture, color, nutritional content, and adherence to government and industry standards; and check raw ingredients for maturity or stability for processing, and finished products for safety, quality, and nutritional value.

Confer with process engineers, plant operators, flavor experts, and packaging and marketing specialists to resolve problems in product development; evaluate food processing and storage operations and assist in the development of quality assurance programs for such operations.
Use equipment such as atomic absorption spectrometers, electron microscopes, flow cytometers, or chromatography systems; and program and use computers to store, process, and analyze data.
Evaluate new supplies and equipment to ensure operability in specific laboratory settings; and supervise technical personnel and postdoctoral research fellows.

Develop food standards and production specifications, safety and sanitary regulations, and waste management and water supply specifications; and develop or improve methods and products for controlling or eliminating weeds, crop diseases, or insect pests using knowledge of chemistry, microbiology, and other sciences.

Conduct research to determine best methods of planting, spraying, cultivating, harvesting, storing, processing, or transporting horticultural products; and conduct experiments investigating how soil forms, changes, or interacts with land-based ecosystems or living organisms.

Conduct experiments to develop new or improved varieties of field crops, focusing on characteristics such as yield, quality, disease resistance, nutritional value, or adaptation to specific soils or climates; and investigate responses of soils to specific management practices to determine the use capabilities of soils and the effects of alternative practices on soil productivity.

Study soil characteristics to classify soils on the basis of factors such as geographic location, landscape position, or soil properties; and investigate soil problems or poor water quality to determine sources and effects.

Communicate research findings on various types of diseases to health practitioners, policy makers, and the public; and educate healthcare workers, patients, and the public about infectious and communicable diseases, including disease transmission and prevention.

Develop new products and procedures for sterilization, food and pharmaceutical supply preservation, or microbial contamination detection; and monitor and perform tests on water, food, and the environment to detect harmful microorganisms or obtain information about sources of pollution, contamination, or infection.

Study methods to improve aspects of foods, such as chemical composition, flavor, color, texture, nutritional value, and convenience; and conduct research investigating transformations of substances in cells, using atomic isotopes.

Provide scientific direction for project teams regarding the evaluation or handling of devices, drugs, or cells for in vitro and in vivo disease models and verify all financial, physical, and human resources assigned to research or development projects are used as planned.
Communicate research or project results to other professionals or the public or teach related courses, seminars, or workshops.
Keep abreast of new biochemistries, instrumentation, or software by reading scientific literature and attending professional conferences.
Identify and analyze public health issues related to foodborne parasitic diseases and their impact on public policies, scientific studies, or surveys.
Investigate the cause, progress, life cycle, or mode of transmission of diseases or parasites.
Conduct experiments regarding causes of bee diseases or factors affecting yields of nectar or pollen.
Investigate insect distribution or habitat and recommend methods to prevent importation or spread of injurious species.

Study the structure and composition of food or the changes foods undergo in storage and processing; and develop new or improved ways of preserving, processing, packaging, storing, and delivering foods.

Confer with health departments, industry personnel, physicians, and others to develop health safety standards and public health improvement programs; and consult with and advise physicians, educators, researchers, and others regarding medical applications of physics, biology, and chemistry.

Study animal and human health and physiological processes; conduct research to examine the molecular or chemical aspects of immune system functioning, and provide advice regarding the development of regulatory standards for land reclamation or soil conservation.
Supervise professional, technical, and clerical personnel; and coordinate molecular or cellular research activities with scientists specializing in other fields.
Provide statistical and computational tools for biologically based activities, such as genetic analysis, measurement of gene expression, or gene function determination.

Prepare summary statistics of information regarding human genomes; participate in all levels of bioproduct development, including proposing new products, performing market analyses, designing and performing experiments, and collaborating with operations and quality control teams during product launches.

Plan or supervise waste management programs for composting or farming; supervise land conservation or reclamation programs for industrial development projects; and study ways to improve agricultural sustainability, such as the use of new methods of composting.
